---
title: "Getting started on ip34 (Mammouth)"
author: "Jonathan Rondeau-Leclaire / Laforest-Lapointe Lab"
date: "Last updated: `r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    toc_depth: 3
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    number_sections: true
    css: dada2_16S_tutorial.css
---

```{r, echo = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE, echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)

klippy::klippy(c('r', 'bash'), color = 'lightblue', tooltip_message = 'Click to copy', position = c('top', 'right'), tooltip_success = 'Done')
```

<div class="callout-note">
**About this guide**  
I show you how to access UdeSherbrooke's ip34 (aka Mammouth) supercomputer. This node is only accessible to UdS students and requires DRAC sponsorship. 

Steps 1, 2 and 4 only need to be done once. 
</div>

---

# Create a DRAC account

To work on ip34, you need a [Digital Research Alliance of Canada](https://ccdb.alliancecan.ca/security/login) account. Ask your director for their sponsorship code. For help, visit their [FAQ](https://docs.alliancecan.ca/wiki/Frequently_Asked_Questions_about_the_CCDB/fr).

---

# Initial setup (locally) 

<div class="callout-danger">
You only need to do this once, ever!
</div>

## Local configuration {.tabset}
This will periodically send a signal to ip34 to make sure you don't spontaneously get logged out. _You only need to do this once._

### On a Mac

Open the Terminal app and run the following:

```{bash mac-ssh}
touch ~/.ssh/config
echo "Host *" >> ~/.ssh/config
echo ServerAliveInterval 60 >> ~/.ssh/config
```


### On Windows

1. Navigate to the `.ssh` program in your home folder, typically under `C:\Users:\<username>\.ssh`
2. Open the config file; if there is none, right-click inside the .ssh folder and select New > Text Document
3. Add the following lines to the file:

```
Host ip34.ccs.usherbrooke.ca
    ServerAliveInterval 60
```

Save it and quit. Then, open the PowerShell app and proceed with the next steps.

## {-}

<div class="callout-note">
If you need a quick primer on the bash language syntax, which is the language the code blocks below are written in, checkout Mike Lee's [awesome tutorial](https://astrobiomike.github.io/unix/unix-intro).
</div>

## Setting up DUO 2FA

All alliance supercomputers require 2-factor authentification using the DUO app. Follow [these steps](https://docs.alliancecan.ca/wiki/Multifactor_authentication#Registering_factors) to set it up.

---

# Logging on to ip34

Whenever you want to access ip34, run this command with your DRAC username (will prompt for password; nothing shows when you'll write your password, that's normal!). Here, use the username to your DRAC account (not your email), for example:

```{bash login}
ssh username@ip34.ccs.usherbrooke.ca
```

You will be prompted with 2FA, which will look like:

```
Enter a passcode or select one of the following options:
1. Duo Push to Pixel8 (Android)
Passcode or option (1-1):
```

Type 1, press return, and you will receive a notification to approve on your phone.

Once you are logged in, you'll see something that looks like this:

```
################################################################################
 __  __      ____  _     
|  \/  |_ __|___ \| |__   Bienvenue sur Mammouth-Mp2b / Welcome to Mammoth-Mp2b
| |\/| | '_ \ __) | '_ \ 
| |  | | |_) / __/| |_) | Aide/Support:    mammouth@calculcanada.ca
|_|  |_| .__/_____|_.__/  Globus endpoint: computecanada#mammouth
       |_|                Documentation:   docs.calculcanada.ca
                                           docs.calculcanada.ca/wiki/Mp2

Grappe avec le meme environnement d'utilisation que Cedar et Graham (Slurm scheduler).
Cluster with the same user environment as Cedar and Graham (Slurm scheduler).

________________________________________________________________________________
          |                               |   Slurm |   Slurm |   Slurm
 Mp2b 	  | Memory/Cores 	                | --nodes | --mem   | --cpus-per-task
 nodetype |                               |   max   |   max   |   max
----------+-------------------------------+---------+---------+-----------------
 base     |  31 GB memory, 24 cores/node  |   1588  |   31G   |   24  
 large    | 251 GB memory, 48 cores/node  |     20  |  251G   |   48
 xlarge   | 503 GB memory, 48 cores/node  |      2  |  503G   |   48
__________|_______________________________|_________|_________|_________________
```
When you see `>`at the bottom, that means Mammouth is ready to receive commands.

<div class="callout-danger">
**Next, you must** execute the following command, **EVERY TIME** you log in.
</div>

```bash
newgrp def-ilafores
```
Otherwise, any file you create will only be readable and writable by you. Do not add the newgrp command to your bashrc, tempting as it may be.

---

# Initial setup (ip34)

Add a few lines to your `.bashrc` file, which is executed every time you login.
<div class="callout-danger">
You only need to do this once, ever!
</div>

```{bash bashrc}
# Sets up certain permissions: 
echo umask 007 >> ~/.bashrc

# Allows access to certain programs (e.g. cutadapt):
echo export MUGQIC_INSTALL_HOME=/cvmfs/soft.mugqic/CentOS6 >> ~/.bashrc 
echo 'module use $MUGQIC_INSTALL_HOME/modulefiles'  >> ~/.bashrc			

# Create a $ILAFORES variable:
echo export ILAFORES=/jbod2/def-ilafores >> ~/.bashrc

# Execute the .bashrc (happens every time you log in, but we need those variables now!)
. ~/.bashrc 
```

<div class="callout-note">
Each command with `>>` appends something to the `.bashrc` file. If you do them multiple times, you'll be adding multiple lines to it.
</div>

This setup will allow others within your group (def-ilafores) to write/execute files you've created, allow you to access certain programs on mugqic, and finally will create a simple variable you can use to navigate to the def-ilafores directory, which is where you should generally work from. To get there and, for example, create a new project (under the analysis folder would be the best place), I suggest adding another variable to your `.bashrc` that will let you use the path to your project directory without having to type it in its entirety every time.

In the following, replace `your_project_name` with the name you want your project directory to have, and `PROJECT1` by a short name you'll remember (it will create a variable pointing to your project directory path, which you can use in place of the path when using the command line).

```{bash project}
# Makes sure the variable exists whenever you log in:
echo "export PROJECT1=$ILAFORES/analysis/your_project_name" >> ~/.bashrc

# execute the bashrc:
. ~/.bashrc

# create your project directory:
mkdir -p $PROJECT1
```

Then, whenever you log in to ip34, using the project directory variable name you've chosen (here PROJECT1), all you have to do is

```{bash daily}
newgrp def-ilafores  # don't forget! ;-)
cd $PROJECT1
```

And you can start doing science!

<div class="callout-note">
Check out [this cool tutorial](https://astrobiomike.github.io/unix/unix-intro) to learn how to get around a little more using this language (bash). Calcul Québec  also regularly give 3-hour workshops on command line (bash) programming for Linux. Check out [their offer](https://www.calculquebec.ca/services-aux-chercheurs/formation/catalogue/).
</div>
---

# What's next? {.tabset}

If you are working with amplicon data, next steps would be to

1. Setup your R environment on ip34 (below)
2. Follow [the DADA2 tutorial](https://jorondo1.github.io/mgx.tutorials/dada2_16S_tutorial.html)

## Setting up the R environment

In microbial ecology, when using R, many packages are often required. Packages are bundles of functions that make coding easier, developed by people like you and me around the world. Installing packages on ip34 can sometimes be a bit problematic, so we have a shared library you can copy into your environment and then build on top of it or update depending on your requirements. To copy it:

```{bash r-libs}
mkdir -p ~/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.4
cp -r $ILAFORES/R_libs_20260401/* ~/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.4
```

Then, load R and activate it.

```{bash r-load}
module load StdEnv/2023 r/4.4.0 mugqic/cutadapt/2.10
nice R
```
<div class="callout-ip34">
The cool thing about ip34 is that there are 96 cores available. So whenever the function you run has a `threads` or `cores` argument or something like that, you can choose how many to use.

Keep in mind that you are not alone on ip34! It is generally frowned upon to huddle all the cores. Try to limit yourself to 24 for most purposes. If you need more, there are 1500 computing nodes that can be used, but this requires a little extra legwork in terms of scripting (but you're having fun so that shouldn't be an issue!).
</div>

<details>
<summary>
what's nice?
</summary>
`nice` is recommended so your program is "nice to others" who use the same resources (so you don't hoard all the cores in ip34). Once you run this, you are now in the equivalent of an R console, which accepts R commands (don't write in bash!). The `>` at the beginning of a new line expects commands in the R language. You'll need to install the packages you need (only once), as you once did on your computer. By default, your current directory will be the one you were in when you activated R.
</details>

## Making stuff run in the background

Imagine you want to run DADA2's sample inference on hundreds of samples... that might take a while! If you want to go home and let it run while you binge-watch Friends, you can either let your laptop running (not recommended), or use a cool little tool called `tmux` directly on ip34. Essentially, tmux creates a virtual shell that runs in the background. If you close your ip34 session (or just log out), it keeps running. Within that tmux shell, you can run anything like you would on your normal shell. To get it running, log in to ip34, get where you need to be, then run:

```{bash tmux-new}
tmux new -s your_session_name
```

The text will disappear on your shell and a green line will appear at the bottom. That's it, you are in a tmux session! You can do anything you would normally have, like load your R environment and execute some commands. Once your code is running, you can log out by using the shortcut `Ctrl+B` on your keyboard, then pressing `D`. `Ctrl+B` lets you send one command to control tmux from within a tmux session, and here we use `D`, which means to Detach from the tmux session. You'll be back to where you were before creating the tmux session. You can check open sessions by doing

```{bash tmux-ls}
tmux ls
```

You can reattach to a session using its name:

```{bash tmux-attach}
tmux attach -t your_session_name
```

And you'll see your code is still running.

<div class="callout-note">
To learn more about tmux (like how to see multiple sessions simultaneously), check out [this blog post](https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/introduction-tmux-linux).
</div>
# {-}


#### Coming soon: 

- metagenomic data processing pipelines
- relative abundance matrix analyses
